title: 2025/05/14 - MA
displayTitle: 2025-05-14 - Māori Affairs Committee - Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlemen
topic: Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlemen
description: 14 May 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements Controller and Auditor-General John Ryan, Auditor-General Andrew McConnell, Deputy Auditor-General Leeanne McAviney, Assistant Auditor-General, Sector Performance Evaan Aramakutu, Manager, Sector Performance Ethan McKenzie, Senior Performance Auditor Lyndsey Gibson, Sector Manager
descriptionSummary: Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlemen. Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty...

chunks.text: Uh, warm welcomes to you all. Uh, thank you very much, uh, coming along, uh, this morning. Welcome, John. Um, it just says a, a little bit of a, a, a, a start to this conversation that clearly we've, uh, got a couple of reports. One is importantly your one, but also we have, uh, Tata report with us, uh, as well.

And we are as a committee, 50 K with regards to discussion. But, uh, a very good read, take it as read, uh, and you've got your, your recommendations in John, I'll, I'll leave it to you how you wish to handle, uh, presenting the report you've provided to us.

Sokoto. Thank you for asking me along. Do you know Lindsey well? Uh, Andrew McConnell, deputy Auditor General, uh, also an officer of Parliament. So, uh, we, we are here today and a team of people including Dan n whose team, uh, produced the report, uh, and, and the team's there. So we've got everyone in and, good.

I didn't realize we had that many ties in the office. So, um, uh, there's obviously the tire supplies being dropped, uh, for this particular meeting. I, I would like to say a few things. I know you've had a chance to read it, but I just feel like I want to just express some things in this report that really sure. Affected me as well. Um, uh, I think, uh, and I, we've done quite a lot of media, and this is quite interesting actually, that there has been such media interest in this.

But I would say this is one of the most important reports of my term, um, seven years in my term. Uh, I think for lots of reasons. Firstly, the accountability, uh, of public agencies is at the base of this report. Um, this is a legal compliance issue if you just take everything else away from it. Fundamentally, this is the law and we should be complying with it as public agencies.

There's also a lot of money, uh, 2.7 billion, and a lot of risk about settlements. Uh, and taking all that aside, there's also the constitutional significance of the treaty settlement process and the implementation of settlements that have been made. So for lots of reasons, it's important. And also when we saw what we saw, um, you know, it, it just, uh, you feel like there things need to be done here.

Uh, so I just want to say that the Auditor General is normally a cold-hearted accountant, but when I read this, I thought, um, uh, I felt like, uh, we needed to act on this. Um, I think the, uh, the other thing that you would be very conscious of, um, you know, the treaty settlement process, um, fee of settlement legislation, uh, historical account apology, redress, various forms of redress.

But the expectation that this is about resetting relationships, uh, I think was really one of the other big things that came through for me. Uh, I've been tangentially involved in treaty settlements for some time as a public servant, but actually that idea of resetting relationships that was, you know, cabinet expectations around this, which have been endured between multiple governments, uh, what we looked at was eight agencies.

There are about 70% of the commitments that have been made through treasury settlements, um, uh, including central and local government agencies and three particular settlements. We looked at the, the lens through that, but we talked to a lot of agencies. And so the issues in this report, I think we can say are widespread. Um, I thought I'd just do two things.

One, just quickly summarize some of the findings, and then perhaps just a little bit about my reflections on those findings. And then, uh, a little bit about call to action. So if you can bear with me for a few minutes, five minutes, maybe, uh, what we found, well, there were some good, there was some good practice we found. Um, so it's, uh, it's good to be able to say that.

But over the time that treaty settlements have been in place, which is now 30 years, and some of these are 10 years old, right? These are not done yesterday, agencies hadn't really got their heads around what the commitments were, the complexity of those commitments.

Uh, they need to work across agency sometimes to deliver on those commitments, and then, of course, to actually deliver the redress. So at the basic level, there wasn't enough understanding. Mm-hmm. The rights of first refusal, we call out, particularly because that's a major part of redress and, and, and settling, uh, settling, uh, and the fact that those systems memorials weren't necessarily well managed or in place in all cases.

And some properties have been sold that should have had a right at first refusal when they found, when Lynn's looked at a number of agencies, a number of, uh, memorials that needed to be put in place. I think the weakness of oversight is another real issue. Um, the support for es, the expectation on es, the reporting to ministers themselves and to parliament about progress of seafood and just old fashioned accountability for non-delivery that we would expect in legal commitments.

There's already been tens of millions, uh, paid in compensation for, um, redress that couldn't be delivered as intended. And there have been some improvements. Tara put in place, Hey, UA and teta, which is the, uh, portal. So it's not that there hasn't been some action and attention to this, but we also point out in the report, they, uh, there's more work to be done in both those areas.

Uh, particularly one in five commitments are not updated because they're outside core government. They're in, um, SOEs, crown Reserve, et cetera. Uh, if I was to step back a little bit and talk about my reaction, uh, to this, uh, a few things, um, if this was tax compliance, imagine that, you know, so why is it that tax compliance is really treated this seriously, but this is equally legislative compliance just as important, probably more important in many ways, um, in terms of constitutional significance,

although I don't wanna say tax compliance isn't important as auditor general. It's very important. I think the other thing, second reaction I might have had is this concept of settlement. And I think when you come at it in a sense, maybe, and I can talk about that, you kind of think of a property transaction. 'cause that's where you hear the word settlement, where you do the business and then you both go your separate ways.

That's not what these are. These are settlements of wrongs. They're resetting a relationship. And the crown, I think, has seen this after the treaty settlement process as a series of transactions that needed to be managed rather than a relationship that needed to be reset.

These are meant to be durable. These are called settlements for a reason. Right? And I think what we've seen here is there is a risk, and Tara ti has made that point itself. There's a risk to the durability of settlements and the goodwill that is built through many, many years of moving towards a settlement process. There's a risk to durability.

And that goodwill that's built through the settlement process by not implementing the settlements as expected was required. And my other reaction was just this tolerance to non-delivery. Firstly, the lack of visibility of non-delivery, and then whether explicit or implicit a tolerance for not delivering.

And that, you know, we can't expect that this is a legal compliance at the, at a space. Right? And I think if I was to step back and think about my children, um, uh, that this could be a massive opportunity, but we risk losing it. We risk losing it by not implementing these settlements, both in terms of the actual listed redress, that's clear enough in the settlement process, but in the reset relationships that comes with.

And so there's a massive opportunity for the country here that these, this report, I think, points to being risk. Um, we make some call to action, uh, in here, um, some recommendations, obviously. But if I was to, to think about this committee, um, and to take it a bit out of my emotional response to my, um, auditor, general cold-hearted response, um, uh, parliament being fully updated with this.

Now, I know there's a, um, on a report on, uh, on progress, but as we point out in the report, sometimes you might see one commitment that hasn't been met, but that could be the fundamental essence of a settlement, and it might just count as one. So, you know, understanding just how complex and difficult settlements can be.

Talking to the post settlement governance entities, I think is really, you know, we've talked through this process. Um, I think that from what I can gather, they've read this report and see that it reflects their concerns. Every agency had some issue in dealing with a settlement, implementing a settlement.

Every PSGE has had some sort of issue as well. So I think hearing from them would round out your perspectives on this report. And, uh, land information. New Zealand clearly has got a big role here and just being, uh, clear that they're, they're on top of the rights of first refusal, those sort of things, memorials, uh, and then TPK, which of course is now transitioning, uh, activity from in this space to help public sector deliver.

Uh, and of course, this committee has a footprint across other committees, and these commitments have footprints in other committees. So whether this committee wants to write us by the committees, pointing out some of the things you would like them to, to be focused on as well. Hmm. So that was kind of my, uh, factual summary and my emotional reaction to this.

Um, I think it is, as I said at the start, um, up there with the most important, uh, reports in my team. Um, look forward to a conversation with you about that. Thank you very much, John. And if I may just start, uh, the conversation, uh, I suppose that the, uh, the situation that we're in today, uh, you could point to many factors, and you've pointed to a lot of that there.

One question I have is, is around the resourcing of the work of mahi that needs to be done on the many fronts to be on assessment. And this is, this is not a today, uh, government thing. This is a, a, a pan, uh, term pan period of time. Um, have we underestimated as the government, as to the resourcing of the post settlement era to be able to achieve the success that we're aiming for?

So most of this work was looking at a period before the last budget and the cycle and the economic cycle. And now, so this is looking at a time, uh, before that mostly, but it looks now. Uh, so that's the first point. The second point I would make on that is coming back to my core here, these are legal commitments and they should be a priority for agencies.

And that, I think means more than just simple resourcing. It means good governance. It means good reporting. It means transparency when things have gone along, it means acting on those issues and trying to deal with it quickly. So I think if you were looking at an agency and its prioritization is really the question. Mm-hmm. And I would've said, this should be a core priority. And we see things like, um, uh, crown forest lease land, which they were given five years to, and I think we mentioned 23, that this the five year, wouldn't it?

And that's 'cause they started late. They thought it was simpler than it was. Right. And that points to governance and internal, uh, processes inside agency. So, uh, you know, it's resource seems a separate question to my mind. This is about prioritization inside agencies. So we talked to that prior. Sorry, I'll, I'll talking to the prioritization, um, to drive the right incentives.

Um, and organization is driven from the top with regards to a, a, a chief executive, et cetera. And you, you talked to that in the recommendation, but just talk to me a bit more about what public service needs to do better to position the mindset of, uh, the, the person at the top of an organization to deliver, be incentivized to deliver that.

'cause you talk about, uh, quite rightly, uh, financials and all this sort of stuff, and that they're held very tightly accountable to the delivery of that as an outcome, but not potentially in this space here. So I'm, I'm talking about, uh, uh, KPIs. I'm talking about agreements in place for employment, all that sort of stuff. Could you talk to that?

Yeah, I think a couple of thoughts on that I might ask, um, Leanne also to just add some comment as well in that space. Uh, so I think we saw that across public sector agencies. Was it two chief executives might have had a performance metric in their performance agreement around, um, managing the treaty settlement post, um, settlement processes.

Um, so we think that the Public Service Commission could turn its mind to, uh, how it wishes to incentivize performance, uh, in this space. I think the other question, uh, is that they, you know, chief executives will have many, many responsibilities and many capabilities, but wanna make sure they also have capability in this space. So some agencies have many thousands of commitments, so that should be a core capability for that chief executive, and they should be supportive in delivering that.

So I think that's a couple of the points we make in the report. Um, you know, turning the mind to how you incentivize the system. But then I think there's a broader question of the system being accountable. So what level of transparency do ministers get? What level of transparency do US Parliament see in this space? So I think you've got a few layers in that question.

Um, but yes, I think chief executives are critical in the space. They need to make sure they have the capability to do that. And we suggest the public service have a, have a thinking that in the, in the recommendations. But lean, I'm sure there's other things. No, I I think your comments are, are being on John. I mean, I think the other thing that we heard for senior leaders in the, um, in the public sector was that hadn't necessarily fully appreciated how much of their role would be needing to grapple with these kinds of issues.

And I think they didn't necessarily feel like they had, um, the right level of support around them to deal with what are quite complex issues. So there's some commentary in the report, and I, I think we, um, suggest that Public Service Commission needs to turn their mind to how they might be to support chief executives as well. Brilliant. Open up to others questions.

If I might, maybe a bit of a, um, supplementary, and thank you very much for the very, um, honest, uh, uh, feelings that you, you shared. Um, Mr. Ryan, uh, what I'd like to ask please, is when we're talking about the, um, the, the something as as simple as the right of first refusal, can you please, um, share with us from your, uh, research, how did, how did so many agencies miss this?

I, I understand we're saying that, that a lot of them don't know, um, they haven't prioritized. But how, how is something so crucial being missed, so missed so often? That's something that blew me away. Uh, I'll ask the team to add some detail to my general comments.

Um, firstly, uh, when I first heard that I had that same reaction that this seems straightforward, but actually putting memorials on titles can be quite complex. Titles can also change. And so they need to know that once the title's moved, where does the right of first refusal, uh, attached to that? Sometimes settlements apply rights of first refusal to a whole region or a whole area of the country.

So they need to work through that quite methodically and quite carefully. Um, and what we are saying in this report is actually that's been, um, not enough attention has been put on that issue and the complexity of that. So often Lins has to work with another agency, so they might issue a new title for some reason, and both agencies need to know that needs a right or first refusal on it.
Um, so those sorts of things fell through the cracks sometimes. But I dunno, there's others with conflict with more understanding Anything.

Uh, and I, my name's Ethan McKenzie, um, I was the audit lead for this piece of work. Um, the only thing I would add is that, um, in terms of the reason why, um, agencies have, uh, kind of mixed understanding of FRS rights of First Refusal is partly because, um, the internal records that some agencies keep of, uh, the titles that that applies to and, um, how those titles might change over time.

Um, obviously there's complexity there because there are a lot of titles that some agencies have to manage. Um, but those records weren't always, um, in the most UpToDate fashion, which is why the memorials can be quite important. It's an important layer of assurance so that if for whatever reason the agency doesn't have a complete record, there is something else on the title itself that will signal to the agency that an RFR applies.

Um, yeah, that's the only thing I wanted to add, But you're right, we talked in the report of many hundreds of those having to be put on over time. Mm-hmm. Thank you. I'm, uh, I'm mindful that that, uh, central government agencies, uh, are a key component of this, but there's others that are outside and you do talk to that you could you, could you talk to the challenge of the Crown being able to have influence over the right outcomes there?

Well, uh, one in five of those commitments that are put through tricky settlements, uh, applies to non-core Crown. Some of those are closer to the Crown. So you do have Crown entities, SOEs, those sorts of agencies which are in the border crown. And then you also have local government. So the complexity is, of course, those various layers of government and how you might mandate across those.

Um, so I think that's one, uh, area of complexity. Uh, but the point we make in here is actually they're a critical part of the overall settlement process. And so making, making transparent the status of those is equally as important. And we just say that, um, you know, uh, TPK should turn its mind to how we expand the reporting across all those, um, different entities.

Um, there might be other that can talk about it, but it's generally the layers of, um, authority that you've got at central government. Yeah. 'cause I'm, I'm mindful that settlements are between Maori and the Crown. Mm. And, uh, it, it'll be easy for the Crown to just say, well, these other entities, I know what they have to do, they should work.

But I, I don't think it should be left to that simple outcome. I think it needs to be more ownership by the crown of what they're actually doing to complete. I think you making exactly the point we hear from the post settlement government entities, they, these are sometimes really small organizations dealing with this massive crown, and the Crown has done this big settlement.

You know, we've been in parliament, we've got the law, and then it fractures into all the agencies that are delivering it. And it's very hard for those ps those post settlement governance entities to actually get this picture across it all. Teta is helping that, that portal, but it doesn't include all the status, uh, updates. And so Absolutely. Right.

One of the things we had time and again, was it shouldn't be us connecting the crown up. The crown should be connecting itself back up. Yeah. Hannah, were you supplementary to that? Okay. Yeah. Uh, thank you. Um, fantastic piece of work. Thank you very much. My question, uh, is a supplemental to Davids and really, um, is asking the question about how the operational delivery of that on the frontline.

So some of those, uh, crown agencies who have, uh, partnership agreements and they're signed, you know, here in Wellington, um, and then they're operationalized. And the tension between the frontline delivery of agencies and post settlement governments entities, uh, sometimes is difficult. 'cause sometimes the frontline workers, they don't, don't understand they have the agreement.

So how, how is that able to be? Was that an issue? Did that come through from the, um, PSGs? I think one of the things we say in the report, and Ethan or Liam might add to it, is, um, agencies actually understanding their commitments fundamentally. Mm-hmm. And then governing them, like they are a commitment, you know, governing them and, and, and their leadership team and through the organization.

So they've line out behind what their commitments are. And I think that was, that's kind of one of the key recommendations. And he, the chief executives for the commitments to actually govern them well, which then does give you that slow down Yeah. End to end, um, commitment inside the agency. I don't know. Well, I was just gonna add to that, I think in, in addition, thinking about the settlement is a whole thing.

So agencies that have individual commitments, having an awareness of what commitments other agencies have and, and doing some work to coordinate with one another to make it a bit easier for post settlement governance entities to engage. Um, you know, uh, Ethan, um, we talked in the report a bit about sometimes a post settlement governance entity is telling one agency what another agency has done wrong, and there didn't seem to be awareness of that all of the time.

So, you know, those things just make it, I think, very difficult to build good, strong relationships that can give post settlement government entities confidence that the, that, that the Crown is doing what it needs to be doing. Mm-hmm. Ethan, I dunno if you want to add, Uh, no. Okay. Tim, um, just wanna to you for, uh, this report and the mahi that's gone into this.

I think last year I was, um, consistently talking about what does this look like for post settlement entities and Iwi. Um, and so it's the first one done in, you know, we are 30 years down the track, so I just wanted my heat to that. Um, I've got so many questions You might need your own session. Yeah. One of mind actually, seriously.

Um, but, uh, please apologize for my broad and then super detailed questions, but, um, I've got a few questions here. Um, don't really know where to start, but basically, um, this whole idea of once all iwis are settled, what does that look like for New Zealand and what does that look like for, uh, the Crown engaging with Iwi?

And, you know, without this, within this whole report, it's kind of like everyone just kind of splits away when really that's not the, that's not the, it's about an ongoing relationship. And how does that look? That's very broad, but, um, yeah, what are your, the Million Dollar question? Well, you know, I probably a little bit outside the scope of the report.

Yeah. But some, some thoughts from the report. One would be, um, this idea of resetting relationships inside as a consequence of the treaty settlement process. Uh, and what we're saying here, is it really unfair agencies what that means? Yeah. Like they can't really do what they don't understand. Understand. And so what we are saying in here is that TPK could actually lead a piece of work in that space that actually said, this is what it should look like.

Yeah. In the future. This is kind of the relationship and how it should feel when you're looking in at the public at the crown. Uh, and I think, I think that would be a really helpful conversation to have in the public sector as well. Absolutely. You know, to just get their heads around that and it would raise the profile of the joint jointness of the crown again. So I think, I think you're right.

I think that's exactly, um, a question that sits outside when people look back in and say, well, what does this reset look like? Yeah. So TPK we say in here could actually, um, hopefully facilitate that conversation. Yeah. And just to up on that, um, just recently we've been going around to different, uh, PhDs and, and, um, for example, you've got the wa t settlement, and then within that there's the Waikato River Authority.

And then within that there's the tu Mana and they're talking with different, uh, councils. Um, they found it very difficult to grasp onto what is, how can they get support? And it's can be quite complex for them. And they, I don't think, it's not their fault, absolutely not.

But what support can we give to them to understand how important legislation like is and the great cost of not understanding those mechanisms. I'm sure others, others have got views on that. I, I'm not sure we, we talk about some of the council relationships we saw.

Yeah. Uh, we saw what worked well in one case and what was a little bit problematic in another. Yeah. Uh, and, um, one of the things we try and do through this is amplify good practice as well, where we see it. So we will be doing some work in that space, um, just to talk to councils about what we saw.

Um, yeah. Good. Look, I think this report sends us in so many different directions when we look at the things that aren't quite right. But I don't know if others wanted to. Well, I think councils, we, we definitely heard from councils through the work that, you know, they, the men that they didn't necessarily get the same support. This wasn't able to provide them with the kind of support that they had been providing to central government agencies.

So I think they're aware of that. Um, Ethan, you might have some specific No, that was what I was gonna say is that the, the councils we spoke to, um, especially on the how to interpret the settlement side, felt like they, um, didn't get the same level of support and what they just couldn't get the same level of support as they as public organizations and the central government do.

Um, and that's one of the areas where we've got a recommendation to suggest that the same level of support that central government gets should be extended in some way to local government and crown entities as well that also fall outside of that group. Yeah. And one more, sorry, just around, um, making the public sector's job a little bit more easier.

I think there's just a long range of timelines between different iwis who are 30 years down the track and who haven't, which you say that having a cohesive of different iwis who can kind of outline this is where we are, these are our challenges, these are what have, this is what has worked for our iwi outside of a national iwi chairs forum will be helpful.

Now you're putting me in really great. Can I pass on a Question? Look, I think, I think the more that the public sector understands and sees the issues, the better it is. That's probably what I would say. And the more that they can share practice with one another. Yeah. Yeah. So that's something we talk about in the report.

We think there's, you know, there is 30 years of experience out here. Yeah, for sure. That they can hopefully find ways to share that with Help each other. Yeah. Can We move to Adrian for a quick, um, my question is about did you find any, or did the, um, PS sgs mentioned, um, any lost opportunities because of delays?

For example, if you had a delay in transferring a piece of forest land, um, and they wanted to sell that piece of land to get a, a better piece of land. Um, yeah. Did they mention anything? Yeah, There's one story in there and I'll just ask Ethan or Leanne just to reflect the story because it was very much in that space. Yeah, Yeah. Um, so we heard, um, an example of, uh, the Crown forestry license land, um, Riverhead Forest, which is just north of Auckland, so in RO here.

Um, and um, long story short, um, that title was meant to transfer to Te Amma within a five year timeframe. And it wasn't. And, um, Teko, Amma, uh, engaged lens about that. And essentially the two, um, organizations kind of came to an agreement about, um, how to recognize that failure.

And part of that, uh, agreement was recognition of the lost opportunity to cover on Maki. Um, and they'll be better placed to explain what the lost opportunity was specifically. But at a high level it was, um, lost commercial opportunity. Um, as I understand it, they wanted to develop some parts of the forest that had been cleared, and the fact that they didn't have title was problematic in that space. Um, Because if you get a piece of land that's at the end of it's third rotation and, um, not viable to plant again,

you want to do something else. Yeah. I think that, Uh, uh, did they talk about why it takes five years? Mm-hmm. Always puzzled me. Why, why it takes that long. So the five year timeframe, um, the five year timeframe was set by, um, kind of an early settlements that involved crown forestry license land, and that five year timeframe was to recognize the complexity of transferring some of that land.

So, um, multiple easements, that sort of thing. Yeah. So sometimes, um, a single crown forestry license land block will need to be split between several different pgs and surveying the land forest for a long time. It can be quite complex to do that. They're very large blocks. Um, there are some other technical aspects to that, um, which we write in the report.

Um, so it was to acknowledge that it was also a five-year timeframe to recognize any unexpected complications. Um, so stuff that might've been outside of lenses control that complicated the transfer. And that can include things like easement. So if PSG wants access to the block over another block, that can be something that links to foresee, but that needs to be sorted before the title can be transferred.

Um, and essentially, uh, there was also a kind of unknown unknowns part of it, which was, well, we needed an extra buffer on top of that to make sure that if anything really serious goes wrong, that we could not possibly have predicted that we've got enough time before the statutory obligation timeframe kind of, um, lessons.

Um, and all of those issues in various ways have impacted the, the forest land that we understand has been transferred beyond that five year timeframe. But the other commonality in all those examples is that Lynn's, um, Lynn started the process too late. They treated the five year timeframe as this is how much time we've got, as opposed to we need to start off day one Right.

And make sure that we get it done as quickly as possible. Because once the five years run out, that's problematic for us. And They acknowledged that, yeah, They've acknowledged that now and they've changed their processes, um, to kind of learn from that. And they now have a, yeah, a different approach to transferring CFLs that, um, that means, in fact, um, in more recent years, they've transferred CFLs quite quickly.

Um, The rental income comes across that, isn't it? One other question is, uh, around relativity. So, um, in claims, yeah, it's an important component, um, and realistically couldn't, um, compare the relativity of a 2025 claim with a, um, a 30-year-old planner.

But that's what the process is. Um, did anyone raise that? 'cause not all claims are equal. You know, you, you've got some settlements that have government interaction at a ministerial level every year. And so they have the opportunity to discuss, um, the issues of, um, um, you know, settlement mechanisms not being delivered.

Um, but not every settlement has those. Mm-hmm. That's right. Did that come up Well, one of the issues that we also point out is, um, things like intro letters of introduction and some of the relationship agreements were actually things that hadn't been delivered. So actually, um, that came up through the sort of aspects of non-delivery you like, rather than necessarily the benefits of having those in place, but just not being put in place was one of the issues that we might like.

Yeah. But, but other than that, though, we didn't, um, comment on, or it's outside of our mandate, I guess, to, to assess differences in settlements and kind of any innovations that would've happened between settlements from 10 years ago and today. One thing that we, we do point out is that, um, the complexity for organizations has increased since the first settlement was settled.

So that's not just because of the number of commitments increasing. Um, so, you know, the more you have to do, the more complex the, the task becomes. But also because, um, and I think this goes to your question, um, also because, uh, some commitments are unique and have been innovated kind of for that settlement, um, for the first time.

Um, which means that agencies have to kind of, or should consider changing their way of working to kind of accommodate that new type of commitment. Well, one is the, uh, right of first refusal.
